channel.title: AI & ML with Don
channel.description: A commute-friendly series rebuilding the fundamentals of AI and machine learning, one episode at a time.
channel.itunes:author: Don Lawson
channel.itunes:summary: A commute-friendly series rebuilding the fundamentals of AI and machine learning, one episode at a time.

item.title: Episode 1: What Machine Learning Actually Is
item.description: Aria and Helio rebuild the foundation: why machine learning flips rules-plus-data into data-plus-answers, the nested circles of AI, ML, and deep learning, the three styles of learning, the predict-loss-gradient-descent training loop and why it reshaped the chip industry, and why generalization beats memorization.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio rebuild the foundation: why machine learning flips rules-plus-data into data-plus-answers, the nested circles of AI, ML, and deep learning, the three styles of learning, the predict-loss-gradient-descent training loop and why it reshaped the chip industry, and why generalization beats memorization.

item.title: Episode 2: The Neuron, Building Block of Neural Networks
item.description: Aria and Helio open the box on the artificial neuron: inputs and weights as importance dials, the weighted sum, the bias as a default leaning, and why activation functions matter — from the untrainable hard step, to the sigmoid, to why simple ReLU won. Then how neurons stack into layers and what "deep" really means.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio open the box on the artificial neuron: inputs and weights as importance dials, the weighted sum, the bias as a default leaning, and why activation functions matter — from the untrainable hard step, to the sigmoid, to why simple ReLU won. Then how neurons stack into layers and what "deep" really means.

item.title: Episode 3: How Neural Networks Learn
item.description: Aria and Helio open the engine room: the forward pass, the loss as the single number training hunts to shrink, and backpropagation explained as blame flowing backward through an org chart — plus the reuse trick that made it practical. Then gradient descent and the learning rate, the humble knob that wastes money if too small and blows up if too big.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio open the engine room: the forward pass, the loss as the single number training hunts to shrink, and backpropagation explained as blame flowing backward through an org chart — plus the reuse trick that made it practical. Then gradient descent and the learning rate, the humble knob that wastes money if too small and blows up if too big.

item.title: Episode 4: Deep Learning, What Depth Really Buys You
item.description: Aria and Helio tackle the word at the center of it all — deep. Why depth buys hierarchy (pixels to edges to parts to faces, learned by the network itself), why a shallow net can only match it at absurd size, and the real reason deep learning exploded around twenty twelve: data, parallel GPUs born from video games (the seed of the Nvidia and chip-war story), and humble fixes — ReLU, smart initialization, normalization — that beat the vanishing gradient and kept the learning signal alive all the way down.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio tackle the word at the center of it all — deep. Why depth buys hierarchy (pixels to edges to parts to faces, learned by the network itself), why a shallow net can only match it at absurd size, and the real reason deep learning exploded around twenty twelve: data, parallel GPUs born from video games (the seed of the Nvidia and chip-war story), and humble fixes — ReLU, smart initialization, normalization — that beat the vanishing gradient and kept the learning signal alive all the way down.

item.title: Episode 5: Convolutional Neural Networks, Teaching Machines to See
item.description: Aria and Helio open the black box from Episode 4: how a layer actually finds an edge. They build the convolution operation from scratch, a tiny filter slid everywhere, slide-multiply-sum, explain feature maps, weight sharing, and translation invariance as a baked-in prior, then add ReLU and pooling to assemble a full CNN. In the Wild closes the loop on AlexNet, the 2012 model that ended hand-engineered computer vision, trained on two GTX 580 gaming cards, and the straight line from that grad-student hack to CUDA, export controls, and the chip war.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio open the black box from Episode 4: how a layer actually finds an edge. They build the convolution operation from scratch, a tiny filter slid everywhere, slide-multiply-sum, explain feature maps, weight sharing, and translation invariance as a baked-in prior, then add ReLU and pooling to assemble a full CNN. In the Wild closes the loop on AlexNet, the 2012 model that ended hand-engineered computer vision, trained on two GTX 580 gaming cards, and the straight line from that grad-student hack to CUDA, export controls, and the chip war.

item.title: Episode 6: Recurrent Neural Networks, Giving Machines Memory
item.description: Aria and Helio give the network something a still image never needed: memory. They build the recurrent neural network from the problem up, why sequences (language, speech, anything where order matters) break a feed-forward net; recurrence and the hidden state as a running summary; the same weights reused across time, the time-twin of the convolution weight-sharing from Episode 5; and backpropagation through time. Then the wall: vanishing and exploding gradients over long sequences, why a vanilla R N N forgets the start of a sentence by the end, and how L S T M and G R U gates open a protected lane for memory. In the Wild lands on sequence-to-sequence neural machine translation and Google's 2016 G N M T, the fixed thought-vector bottleneck that attention was invented to break, and why recurrence starving parallel hardware set the stage for the Transformer.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio give the network something a still image never needed: memory. They build the recurrent neural network from the problem up, why sequences (language, speech, anything where order matters) break a feed-forward net; recurrence and the hidden state as a running summary; the same weights reused across time, the time-twin of the convolution weight-sharing from Episode 5; and backpropagation through time. Then the wall: vanishing and exploding gradients over long sequences, why a vanilla R N N forgets the start of a sentence by the end, and how L S T M and G R U gates open a protected lane for memory. In the Wild lands on sequence-to-sequence neural machine translation and Google's 2016 G N M T, the fixed thought-vector bottleneck that attention was invented to break, and why recurrence starving parallel hardware set the stage for the Transformer.

item.title: Episode 7: Attention and the Transformer
item.description: Aria and Helio break the wall Episode 6 left them at: the fixed thought-vector bottleneck and recurrence starving parallel hardware. The fix is attention, a learned, soft lookup, query, key, and value, that lets a model weigh the whole input instead of cramming it through one vector. They build self-attention (how the word it finds what it refers to), the parallelism unlock that finally fits the G P U, and then the Transformer from Attention Is All You Need, twenty seventeen: multi-head attention and positional encoding, recurrence dropped entirely. In the Wild grounds it in the Transformer itself, why it displaced Google's own G N M T, and Don's chip-war read: turning sequence modeling into dense parallel matrix math made progress a function of compute you can buy, the starting gun for the scaling era and the throughline to Nvidia, T P Us, and export controls.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio break the wall Episode 6 left them at: the fixed thought-vector bottleneck and recurrence starving parallel hardware. The fix is attention, a learned, soft lookup, query, key, and value, that lets a model weigh the whole input instead of cramming it through one vector. They build self-attention (how the word it finds what it refers to), the parallelism unlock that finally fits the G P U, and then the Transformer from Attention Is All You Need, twenty seventeen: multi-head attention and positional encoding, recurrence dropped entirely. In the Wild grounds it in the Transformer itself, why it displaced Google's own G N M T, and Don's chip-war read: turning sequence modeling into dense parallel matrix math made progress a function of compute you can buy, the starting gun for the scaling era and the throughline to Nvidia, T P Us, and export controls.

item.title: Episode 8: LLMs, How Modern AI Actually Works
item.description: Aria and Helio take the Transformer from last episode and pour in the fuel. What happens when you train that architecture on enormous quantities of text from the open web? You get a large language model. The recipe is almost embarrassingly simple: predict the next word, at massive scale. They trace the two competing philosophies of twenty eighteen, Open A I's autoregressive G P T (predict the next token, left to right) and Google's B E R T (mask random words and predict them from both sides). G P T scaled up (one point five billion to a hundred seventy five billion parameters), B E R T became the backbone of search. Then the episode builds the economics: scaling laws that made A I progress an engineering problem rather than a discovery problem, emergent capabilities that appear at specific size thresholds, and the compute cost curve from millions to hundreds of millions of dollars per training run. In the Wild grounds it in the chip supply chain: Nvidia, T S M C, A S M L, and why U S export controls on advanced silicon are a direct consequence of the Transformer making frontier A I a hardware problem with physical chokepoints.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio take the Transformer from last episode and pour in the fuel. What happens when you train that architecture on enormous quantities of text from the open web? You get a large language model. The recipe is almost embarrassingly simple: predict the next word, at massive scale. They trace the two competing philosophies of twenty eighteen, Open A I's autoregressive G P T (predict the next token, left to right) and Google's B E R T (mask random words and predict them from both sides). G P T scaled up (one point five billion to a hundred seventy five billion parameters), B E R T became the backbone of search. Then the episode builds the economics: scaling laws that made A I progress an engineering problem rather than a discovery problem, emergent capabilities that appear at specific size thresholds, and the compute cost curve from millions to hundreds of millions of dollars per training run. In the Wild grounds it in the chip supply chain: Nvidia, T S M C, A S M L, and why U S export controls on advanced silicon are a direct consequence of the Transformer making frontier A I a hardware problem with physical chokepoints.

item.title: Episode 9: Embeddings and Retrieval, Vectors as Meaning
item.description: Aria and Helio turn the search problem into geometry: embeddings as vector representations, similarity as the retrieval primitive, and why R A G separates intelligence from memory. They distinguish predictive, contrastive, and supervised training objectives; explain cosine, dot product, approximate nearest neighbor search, and the limits of similarity for freshness, authority, and truth. In the Wild grounds it in YouTube recommendations: candidate generation, ranking, data flywheels, and why retrieval is the product boundary, not background plumbing.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio turn the search problem into geometry: embeddings as vector representations, similarity as the retrieval primitive, and why R A G separates intelligence from memory. They distinguish predictive, contrastive, and supervised training objectives; explain cosine, dot product, approximate nearest neighbor search, and the limits of similarity for freshness, authority, and truth. In the Wild grounds it in YouTube recommendations: candidate generation, ranking, data flywheels, and why retrieval is the product boundary, not background plumbing.

item.title: Episode 10: Training Data and the Data Pipeline
item.description: Aria and Helio explain why data is the supply chain of machine learning: collection, labeling, cleaning, splits, leakage, distribution shift, and the pipelines that keep models useful after launch.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio explain why data is the supply chain of machine learning: collection, labeling, cleaning, splits, leakage, distribution shift, and the pipelines that keep models useful after launch.

item.title: Episode 11: Pretraining vs Fine-tuning
item.description: Aria and Helio separate the giant base-model run from the post-training work that turns a predictor into an assistant: supervised fine tuning, instruction tuning, preference tuning, and R L H F. In the Wild grounds it in Instruct G P T and the strategic lesson that scarce pretraining compute creates capability, while post-training shapes product behavior and distribution.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio separate the giant base-model run from the post-training work that turns a predictor into an assistant: supervised fine tuning, instruction tuning, preference tuning, and R L H F. In the Wild grounds it in Instruct G P T and the strategic lesson that scarce pretraining compute creates capability, while post-training shapes product behavior and distribution.

item.title: Episode 12: Evaluation and Bias
item.description: Aria and Helio explain how teams actually measure model behavior: labels, held-out tests, error tradeoffs, thresholds, calibration, bias, subgroup metrics, and why benchmarks can hide the people a system fails. In the Wild grounds the lesson in the Gender Shades benchmark controversy and the strategic shift from single accuracy claims to accountability by slice.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Aria and Helio explain how teams actually measure model behavior: labels, held-out tests, error tradeoffs, thresholds, calibration, bias, subgroup metrics, and why benchmarks can hide the people a system fails. In the Wild grounds the lesson in the Gender Shades benchmark controversy and the strategic shift from single accuracy claims to accountability by slice.

item.title: Episode 13: Scaling Laws and Inference Economics
item.description: Why scaling laws stay smooth when eval jumps, why training is one-time but inference is forever, and how KV cache, batching, quantization, and chip-supply shape product economics. In the Wild: Llama 405B dense training and DeepSeek V3 MoE, plus power and export controls.
item.itunes:author: Don Lawson
item.itunes:summary: Why scaling laws stay smooth when eval jumps, why training is one-time but inference is forever, and how KV cache, batching, quantization, and chip-supply shape product economics. In the Wild: Llama 405B dense training and DeepSeek V3 MoE, plus power and export controls.
